kanji.meaningMnemonic: "A <radical>scooter</radical>!" you <radical>announce</radical>. You are announcing this because you decided this is what you would <kanji>create</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine being at a maker's contest. Each person has to announce loudly what they'll create, then they have to create it.
kanji.readingMnemonic: But, a scooter has been <kanji>create</kanji>d before. What is different about yours? The scooter you will create is specifically designed to keep <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう) busy.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine the scooter you created with a zombie on it. It's a perfect place for the zombie to get stuck and scooted off into nowhere.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: There are <radical>fingers</radical> reaching towards you asking you to <radical>accept</radical> to <kanji>instruct</kanji> them. They're the fingers of students. And you're the teacher. See them reach out for knowledge. They want to accept this knowledge into their hearts and brains. Instruct them!
kanji.meaningHint: Picture yourself instructing a class full of knowledge hungry little kids. They reach out with their fingers to accept the truth you bring to them.
kanji.readingMnemonic: "Yes! I will <kanji>instruct</kanji> you, my dear students!" you cry, "I will instruct you on how to open a small <reading>ju</reading>ice (じゅ) box!" That's right. These little kids need help learning how to open small juice boxes (small because kids are small).
kanji.readingHint: Picture yourself instructing little kids how to punch the straw into small juice boxes. Hold it right-side-up! Don't squeeze too hard! Don't punch it through the other side! No! Don't bite the box! Instructing is hard!

rawData.meaning_hint: Picture yourself instructing a class full of knowledge hungry little kids. They reach out with their fingers to accept the truth you bring to them.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You take this <radical>comb</radical>, break the handle off, and use it to <radical>stamp</radical> your official <kanji>seal</kanji> of approval onto a document. It's kind of a messy <kanji>mark</kanji>ing process, but it gets the job done.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine breaking and bending a comb so you can use it to stamp your seal onto things.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You need this <kanji>seal</kanji> to stay at an <reading>inn</reading> (いん). All inns require official paperwork, so you had to improvise and make a seal on the fly.
kanji.readingHint: Hopefully the inn accepts this seal... or you aren't going to have a place to sleep tonight. And it kind of just looks like you dipped a comb in ink and pressed it around on some paper.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: In your <radical>warehouse</radical> you use your <radical>knife</radical> to <kanji>create</kanji>. You keep old pieces of wood in your warehouse, and you use your knife to carve them into new things that only you could create.
kanji.meaningHint: What do you create in your warehouse? Picture all the different things you could create in your mind.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you <kanji>create</kanji> it's good for your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). It's so freeing, in fact, that when you get really into your creating, sometimes you'd swear your soul was floating a little bit outside of your body. But every time you notice, it sinks back down. Hmm.
kanji.readingHint: When you create something your soul is happy! It's good for you, you know?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around a <radical>black hole</radical>, waiting for it to finish destroying stuff so you can switch it to <kanji>restore</kanji> mode. You were told all black holes finish eventually, and when they do you can press a button to restore everything back to the way it was.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine loitering around near (but not too near!) the black hole, waiting for it to finish so you can restore the galaxy.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When there's nothing else for the black hole to eat, you hit the <kanji>restore</kanji> button.... but nothing happens. "Oh, <reading>f**k</reading> (ふく) you!" you scream, as you realize you were lied to.
kanji.readingHint: Maybe if you yell f**k you enough things will be restored. Ha. No. Everything's gone and it's your fault for being gullible.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around your <radical>master</radical> until he <kanji>depart</kanji>s on a <kanji>journey</kanji>. You have a lot more freedom to do things after your master has departed, so until that time you just loiter about looking busy and staying out of his way.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture yourself loitering around your master’s fancy mansion. You’re pretending to dust things off, but you’re actually just waiting for him to depart on his journey.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Finally, it's time for your master's <kanji>journey</kanji> and he calls to you and says, "You are the <reading>王</reading> (おう) of the house now. Take care of everything while I'm gone."
kanji.readingHint: Your master has departed! You are the 王 now!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Your new <radical>car</radical> doesn’t <radical>mix</radical> well with the other cars in your garage because its color is in stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to the others. All of your other cars are pristine white in color, but this new car is a mix of lots of colors. It almost looks like a peacock or something it’s so colorful. Such a contrast.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine this garage of yours with a sea of white cars. Now there is a new car that really sticks out like a sore thumb. Imagine yourself looking out over your cars and seeing this wacky car practically jumping out at you.
kanji.readingMnemonic: What did I say this car looks like? Oh that’s right, a pea<reading>cock</reading> (かく). This car looks like a peacock. It’s the color of a peacock and you’ll look like you’re peacocking when you’re out on the town driving it. It really is such a stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to like, every other car out there.
kanji.readingHint: See yourself driving around in this peacock car. Hear the engine purr as strangers jeer and yell at you. This car sure does get a lot of attention, doesn’t it?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Out of <radical>bamboo</radical>, you make a <radical>brush</radical> – a <kanji>writing brush</kanji>. This is no ordinary brush, mind you. It is a brush specifically designed for writing elegant characters. And it’s made from bamboo!
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine yourself using this bamboo writing brush. Do you know many kanji? Of course you do! Imagine yourself writing them all with this nice writing brush. It’s actually fun to write things with it!
kanji.readingMnemonic: Once the <kanji>writing brush</kanji> <reading>hits</reading> (ひつ) that paper, it’s like you transform into a totally different person. All of a sudden you’re a master calligrapher, writing anything and everything in a most beautiful fashion. Once that brush hits the paper, it’s on!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine the sound the brush makes when it hits the paper. It’s a bit of a plop, and then kind of a scratching sound as the brush bristles move around. Really focus on this sound that the brush makes when it hits the paper.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You hold <radical>gold</radical> <radical>fins</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> while shopping for pencil <kanji>lead</kanji> at the store. This pencil lead store only accepts gold fins as payment, but your fins don’t fit in your pockets and you need your hands to carry the lead so the gold fins go in your mouth.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine having these gold fins in your mouth. Taste the metal as your tongue runs over them.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You pay for your <kanji>lead</kanji> but the cashier puts it in your mouth where the gold fins were and... everything <reading>en</reading>ds (えん).
kanji.readingHint: Lead poisoning, huh? What a way for everything to end...

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You keep all of your <radical>shellfish</radical> under a <radical>roof</radical> down the <radical>street</radical> and it's your <kanji>savings</kanji>. Shellfish used to be used as money way back, and these aren't shellfish you're spending, they're ones you're saving up for later.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine walking down the street to deposit more shellfish into your special savings and gaze happily at the huge pile you keep far enough away that you aren't tempted to spend it.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you want to withdraw from your <kanji>savings</kanji> you force yourself to use <reading>cho</reading>psticks (ちょ) to pick up the shellfish. They're especially slippery, hard to use chopsticks too, that way it's really hard to take too much out of your savings.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine trying to lift up a shellfish with a pair of smooth chopsticks. It slips right out. Ugh! I guess that's good though, because it's a savings for a reason (you shouldn't be spending it)!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>old</radical> <radical>winter</radical> is to blame for today's <kanji>circumstance</kanji>. It is the <kanji>reason</kanji> things are bad now.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine a poor, broken town. And, imagine the mayor of a town telling their people that things are bad because of that bad old winter. "It's to blame for our circumstance!" he yells.
kanji.readingMnemonic: In reality, this <kanji>circumstance</kanji> was actually caused by a single <reading>子</reading> (こ), though. This kid went through town, destroying everything in their path.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine your kid character walking through town, using their super powers to destroy buildings and rot food.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>building</radical> standing in the way of the next <radical>chapter</radical> in your life, and it's <kanji>hinder</kanji>ing you. It's not like you couldn't find another way around, but it's definitely inconvenient.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this huge, tall building standing directly in the way. You peer around the other side and there is a book floating in the air that says "Chapter 5: Success and Happiness." Dang, you really want to get over there to that part of your life.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Someone put this building here specifically to <kanji>hinder</kanji> you. They knew you were close to the next chapter of your life, and they wanted to hinder you really good. And there they are, sitting on a throne smirking. It's the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう).
kanji.readingHint: The Shougun wants to hinder you and your life. He wants you to have to work harder so you become better, faster, and stronger than anyone else. Hindering you is his way of making sure you don't take the easy way out in life.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: If you see some entity <radical>loiter</radical>ing with <radical>horns</radical> on its head atop your <radical>coat rack</radical>, you should probably <kanji>obey</kanji> it and <kanji>accompany</kanji> it wherever it leads you.
kanji.meaningHint: First of all, this could be really fun. Second of all, if this entity is a demon or something, you should really just obey it or it might curse you. What's the worst that could happen?!
kanji.readingMnemonic: If you <kanji>obey</kanji> the horned entity atop your coat rack, you'll end up with a ton of <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう)!
kanji.readingHint: Pledging to obey and accompany a mysterious horned entity who lavishes jewels upon you definitely seems like a good decision. Not sketchy at all.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Your <radical>ego</radical>... it's totally out of control. <kanji>I</kanji> mean, my ego isn't out of control. I'm the greatest. That's <kanji>me</kanji>. But your ego? Ugh, so out of control unlike my amazing ego.
kanji.meaningHint: Just keep going with this sentence about the ego. You can't stop, because really it's your ego that's out of control. That's <kanji>I</kanji> when you're saying it.
kanji.readingMnemonic: <kanji>I</kanji> am the greatest in the world! I am no baby like you, that's why. A baby who says "<reading>ga</reading> (が) ga ga ga!"
kanji.readingHint: You're just destroying the ego of that other guy. "Why?" you ask? "Because <kanji>I</kanji> am the boss here. And you are the baby. Ego things."

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> that is <radical>white</radical>, traveling in the <radical>direction</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> is a <kanji>fierce</kanji> and <kanji>violent</kanji> force of nature. This white tsunami is chasing after winter, and that’s why the tsunami is white—it’s a winter tsunami. It’s the most fierce and most violent kind of tsunami in existence.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine this white tsunami, more violent and fierce than any other tsunami you’ve ever seen, coming toward you. It’s very frightening. Feel the cold wetness it brings to your skin.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The tsunami is too <kanji>fierce</kanji>, too <kanji>violent</kanji>, and before you realize what's happening, <reading>Gecki</reading> (げき), your beloved gecko, is lifted up and carried off into the air.
kanji.readingHint: "Geecckkkiiiiii!!!" you scream in horror, as he's thrown about by the fierce, winter tsunami. You have to close your eyes. You can't watch Gecki go.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The "<radical>Cliff</radical> of <radical>Ten Thousand</radical> <radical>Power</radical>" is where you go if you want to be <kanji>encourage</kanji>d.
kanji.meaningHint: If you can get to the top of the cliff, it will use its "ten thousand power" to encourage you.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you get there, it does <kanji>encourage</kanji> you. The cliff says "Hey, I encourage you to buy a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun."
kanji.readingHint: A cliff is encouraging you to buy a ray gun for some reason. This is not the encouragement you expected, but it is encouragement nonetheless. You go back down the cliff and immediately buy a ray gun.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> using a <radical>measurement</radical> device (like a ruler) is a good way to <kanji>chastise</kanji> someone. They do something bad? Chastise them by slapping their wrists, their feet, their eyes, ears, anything! They say something out of turn? CHASTISE THEM!
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine being chastised with a measurement device. Think how horrible it would be getting hit on your knuckles, your nose, or the back of your head. It would be awful. Imagine this experience.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You're being <kanji>chastise</kanji>d because you tried to say something in English in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Everyone around you slowly turned around, eyes aflame and rulers in hand, ready to chastise you for uttering those forbidden words.
kanji.readingHint: How could you say something in English while you're in とうきょう?! After you went to all the trouble to learn all this kanji and Japanese? Geez, we almost think you deserve to be chastised...

rawData.meaning_hint: Imagine being chastised with a measurement device. Think how horrible it would be getting hit on your knuckles, your nose, or the back of your head. It would be awful. Imagine this experience.

Chastise them by slapping their wrists, their feet, their eyes, ears, anything! They say something out of turn? CHASTISE THEM!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>droopy</radical> <radical>building</radical> is where people droop under big sacks of <kanji>mail</kanji>, aka a <kanji>post</kanji> office.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture a bunch of postal workers going in and out of the post office building, drooping under the weight of so much mail. It's a tough job.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Because it's such a physically demanding job, all the <kanji>mail</kanji> carriers in this <kanji>post</kanji> office are <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう). Only the youths can handle this kind of heavy mail-bag lifting, I guess.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine all these droopy youths, showing up for the morning shift to pay their way through college as mail carriers. Ah, you were one of them once…

kanji.meaningMnemonic: In a <radical>gold</radical> <radical>cross</radical>, you see a <kanji>needle</kanji> sticking out of it. The needle is right in the middle of the gold cross. If Jesus were on there, the needle would be sticking him right in the chest.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this golden cross in your head. Why do you suppose there’s this needle stuck in it? You try to pull it out but you can’t. Envision yourself straining, trying to get the thing out.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You're about to give up on the <kanji>needle</kanji> when it pops out and flies directly into your <reading>shin</reading> (しん). Ouch! Now you're desperately trying to pull the needle out of your shin, but it really won't budge this time. Maybe you should have minded your own business.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine a needle sticking into your shin. It hurts a ton. How would you try to get it out?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: A <radical>loiter</radical>ing <radical>mountain</radical> <radical>king</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical> is an <kanji>indication</kanji> or <kanji>sign</kanji> of something to come.
kanji.meaningHint: We don't want to focus too much on what the indication / sign actually is, though, because it will take away from the <kanji>indication</kanji> part (which we want to remember). So, focus on the fact that this is some kind of indication that's important, but you don't know what that indication means or is... because we'll use that with the reading.
kanji.readingMnemonic: When you spot a loitering mountain king in the winter, you know it's an <kanji>indication</kanji> that soon Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) will appear. It's like a Groundhog's Day kind of situation, but instead it means Mrs. Chou is about to come out, perhaps early for the season.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine an announcer watching the loitering mountain king, "There he is... and it's still winter. We all know what that means. It's a sign that Mrs. Chou will be coming out of hibernation soon." Then, you can imagine her bursting forth from the mountain, running down her street.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>soul</radical> floating around a <radical>tombstone</radical> acting <kanji>suspicious</kanji>. To be fair, if a soul is going to be anywhere, a tombstone is probably high up on the list, but this one seems like it's doing something weird.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine inching closer to this suspicious soul floating around a tombstone. What do you think it's doing?
kanji.readingMnemonic:
You sneak around the tombstone to see what this <kanji>suspicious</kanji> soul is doing, and you realize it's pretending to <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい). The soul is sitting behind the tombstone, in a small kayak, moving its arms like it's rowing through water.

Even more suspicious, the soul seems to be holding a <reading>ke</reading>ttle (け). Considering the soul is already dead, this doesn't seem like something it will need.
kanji.readingHint: Picture a suspicious, kettle holding, kayaking soul. The afterlife may be more interesting than you thought.

rawData.meaning_hint: Imagine inching closer to this suspicious soul floating around a tombstone. What do you think it's doing?

kanji.meaningMnemonic: On the <radical>grass</radical> near a <radical>rice paddy</radical> sits a <radical>creeper</radical> that is some sort of a <radical>dog</radical> <kanji>beast</kanji>, a vicious <kanji>animal</kanji>. This creeper isn’t just a normal dog, it is more beast-like, more like a feral animal than domesticated pet.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture this beast creeping, low to the ground, in the grass around the rice paddy. It's got its eyes on you. Uh oh.
kanji.readingMnemonic: The <kanji>beast</kanji> is guarding its precious <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう), which it buried in the grass or the rice paddy somewhere. If you don't want the beast to think you're coming to steal those jewels, you better keep your distance.
kanji.readingHint: All beasts have something they're trying to protect. In this case, it's some beastly jewels.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You take some <radical>flowers</radical> and some <radical>fruit</radical> and you make a <kanji>cake</kanji> out of it. It’s a very nice looking, flavorful cake. The fruit makes it taste great and the flowers make for great cake decorations.
kanji.meaningHint: Look at this cake you have made. It’s majestic. Now taste some of it. It is one great tasting cake. Imagine yourself eating the best cake in all of ever.
kanji.readingMnemonic: You’re standing back, admiring your <kanji>cake</kanji> when you hear a <reading>ca</reading>r (か) engine revving and before you know it your cake has been run over. The car came out of nowhere and ran your cake right over, spraying fruit cake bits and flowers all over the place. Stupid car!
kanji.readingHint: Hear the roar of the car engine as you get splattered in the face with the cake. Wipe it off of your face just in time to see the car speeding off into the distance. Also, recall that this kanji shares a reading with the similar kanji, 果.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: During a <radical>tsunami</radical>, your <radical>lip ring</radical> is ripped off while you’re trying to cross a <kanji>river</kanji>. There was no other path to safety, you had to cross the raging river, but you lost your footing and smashed your face on a rock and lost your lip ring.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine how painful this would be, having your lip ring ripped from your lip. It would hurt a lot. Touch your lip and imagine missing half of it. Yikes.
kanji.readingMnemonic: But why were you trying to cross this <kanji>river</kanji> anyway? Well your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) was on the other side of it, and you figured if you could get to your car you could outrun the tsunami and be safe. Too bad you fell and ripped out your lip ring though.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine yourself fording the river, all wet and cold from the water. Keeping your eyes on your car, you move slowly across. Look at the car. Look at it!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: You're trying to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to cause a <radical>landslide</radical> so you have to <kanji>shake</kanji> the earth. You probably won't succeed, but the best you can do is to shake your hands on the dirt as hard as you can. It looks like you're trying to <kanji>wave</kanji> at the ground now.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine shaking your fingers at the dirt. Do you think you can shake the dirt hard enough to cause a landslide (spoilers, no).
kanji.readingMnemonic: You think you finally got the earth to <kanji>shake</kanji> but when you look down it's just your <reading>shin</reading>s (しん). Darn you shins, I thought I made some progress. Hey wait... why won't you stop?!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine your shins shaking, your hands are waving, and they won't stop. No! You shouldn't have tried to start a landslide. This was a bad idea!

kanji.meaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> going down your <radical>dry</radical> face is just <kanji>sweat</kanji>.
kanji.meaningHint: Sweat really does feel like a tsunami though. Wow, it's dry and hot out right now!
kanji.readingMnemonic: The <kanji>sweat</kanji> isn't just on your face, it's also dripping down your <reading>ass</reading> (あせ).
kanji.readingHint: The one place you wish sweat wouldn't go, but somehow it always does: the ass.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>ground</radical>, a <radical>head</radical> covered in <radical>dirt</radical> emerges... <kanji>again</kanji>! It's not the first time you've seen this buried head, nor is it the last. This dirt-covered head hides beneath the ground wherever you go, and keeps popping up to scare you again and again.
kanji.meaningHint: Picture the dirt-covered head popping up again and again. How can it move under the ground like that if it's just a head? You decide to get closer this time, and for once it speaks: "Get me out of the ground!"
kanji.readingMnemonic: The dirt-covered head asks for help <kanji>again</kanji> and again, but you've only got a <reading>saw</reading> (さ). So you start digging with it, scraping the dirt away, until you see this head has the body of a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine how dirty you and your saw are after all this. At least you could help this cyborg with your saw. "Thanks again," it says, then runs off shaking the dirt out of its hair.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> touch as you <radical>stand</radical> with a <radical>woman</radical> and <kanji>adjoin</kanji> yourselves. You are united, you are one.
kanji.meaningHint: Look around you. Other women are adjoining their fingers and standing with you. There's hundreds, no, thousands of you standing together.
kanji.readingMnemonic: All the women <kanji>adjoin</kanji> in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). Sets of two, of course. One woman with another woman, standing in unison.
kanji.readingHint: Picture a bunch of women standing together. They're holding hands in sets, adjoined together.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: There once was an <radical>animal</radical> with an <radical>insect</radical> friend who felt very <kanji>alone</kanji> unless he was around. An animal and an insect are an unlikely pairing, but these two get along delightfully and hate to be alone without the other.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine this animal and this insect. Let’s say it’s a flea and a dog. Imagine them going everywhere together. To the park, getting ice cream, even the drive in movie theater. They’re inseparable.
kanji.readingMnemonic: One activity that the animal can never do <kanji>alone</kanji> is solve Su<reading>doku</reading> (どく) puzzles. The insect always has to help him out with them, and together they are an unstoppable Sudoku solving team.
kanji.readingHint: Picture this animal with the insect on his head doing a Sudoku. See the insect hopping around on the animals head shouting answers and suggestions into the animal’s ear. How cute.

kanji.meaningMnemonic:
The  <radical>fortune</radical> radical and the <kanji>fortune</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! This can also mean <kanji>occupy</kanji>, maybe because learning your fortune shows you how you're going to occupy your life. Or how it will occupy you…

You haven't seen the radical in a while, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember the kanji: Put your toe in your mouth to learn your <kanji>fortune</kanji>. The taste will tell you everything you need to know about your fortune. But your toe has to <kanji>occupy</kanji> your mouth first for it to work.
kanji.meaningHint: Imagine what your toe tastes like. Or actually taste it, I don't care. What do the taste and texture of it say about you, about your future fortune?
kanji.readingMnemonic: To <kanji>occupy</kanji> some free time you have, you go and get your <kanji>fortune</kanji> told at a fortune teller. Your fortune is that you’ll have an encounter with a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) in the near future. Just then you realize that the fortune teller is actually a centaur! The fortune has come true!
kanji.readingHint: Imagine getting your fortune told by a centaur fortune teller. Do you trust this mythical beast to know your future? Watch him manipulate his crystal ball as his hooves stomp the ground beneath him.

kanji.meaningMnemonic: This kanji comes straight from the <radical>droopy</radical> radical. What do droopy things do? They <kanji>droop</kanji>. Oh, and sometimes they <kanji>dangle</kanji> too.
kanji.meaningHint: Don't let your spirits droop — kanji can be tough to learn, but you're doing great!
kanji.readingMnemonic: You know what's better when it <kanji>droop</kanji>s a little? A <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい). Those swings with the droopy rubber seats are so comfortable to sit on and <kanji>dangle</kanji> your legs off of — way better than the rigid wooden ones.
kanji.readingHint: Imagine setting your bum in a droopy rubber swing seat. Feel it droop down a little lower as you put your weight into it and let your legs dangle. Ah, such comfort.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Needle</kanji> <kanji>medicine</kanji> is when you stick needles into people to cure them. This is done via an <vocabulary>acupuncturist</vocabulary>.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
This word uses the kun'yomi reading for 針 and the on'yomi reading for 医. You haven't learned this reading for 針 yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

If you do <vocabulary>acupuncture</vocabulary> and stick a bunch of needles in your back it's going to look really <reading>hairy</reading> (はり). Needles look just like metal hairs when they're coming out of someone. Just picture hairy little needles all over your body and you'll never forget this reading!

rawData.meaning_mnemonic: <kanji>Needle</kanji> <kanji>medicine</kanji> is when you stick needles into people to cure them. This is done via an <vocabulary>acupuncturist</vocabulary>.

Needles look just like metal hairs when they're coming out of someone. Just picture hairy little needles all over your body and you'll never forget this reading!

context_sentences.en: My younger brother has a dream to be an acupuncturist.
context_sentences.ja: 私の弟には、針医になるという夢があります。

context_sentences.en: I'll send you the phone number of my favorite acupuncturist!
context_sentences.ja: お気に入りの針医の電話番号を送るね！

context_sentences.en: It’s amazing to think that acupuncturist techniques have come this far.
context_sentences.ja: 針医の技術の進歩には驚くばかりです。
